prompt: Write a paper to discuss the influence of AI interaction on interpersonal relations, considering AI's potential to fundamentally change how and why individuals relate to each other.
article:
## Framing the question

**AI interaction is no longer just “using a tool.”** Across recent research, AI now appears in social life in at least two roles: **as a relational partner** people talk to, confide in, or attach to, and **as a relational mediator** that drafts messages, ranks feeds, recommends people and content, monitors behavior, or otherwise shapes human-human interaction from the background [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/) [[2]](https://socialmedialab.sites.stanford.edu/sites/g/files/sbiybj22976/files/media/file/hancock-jcmc-ai-mediated-communication.pdf)(https://socialmedialab.sites.stanford.edu/sites/g/files/sbiybj22976/files/media/file/hancock-jcmc-ai-mediated-communication.pdf). That distinction matters because AI can change interpersonal relations in two fundamentally different ways: it can **become one of the parties** in a relationship, or it can **alter the conditions under which humans relate to each other** [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/) [[2]](https://socialmedialab.sites.stanford.edu/sites/g/files/sbiybj22976/files/media/file/hancock-jcmc-ai-mediated-communication.pdf)(https://socialmedialab.sites.stanford.edu/sites/g/files/sbiybj22976/files/media/file/hancock-jcmc-ai-mediated-communication.pdf).

Recent theory gives this distinction more precision. The MIRA model—machine-integrated relational adaptation—treats current AI systems as technologies that not only deliver information but also co-create interactional experiences. That framing is useful because it separates two different causal problems. In the **partner** case, the central question is why a person grants quasocial standing to a machine. In the **mediator** case, the central question is how AI changes authorship, visibility, timing, tone, and incentives between human beings. These are not variants of the same effect. A lonely user bonding with Character.AI, a teenager using Snapchat’s My AI for advice, a worker using Copilot to soften feedback to a colleague, and a citizen whose political feed is ranked by recommender systems are all “interacting with AI,” but the route from system design to social consequence is different in each case.

A broad definition of **AI interaction** therefore includes at least six classes of systems: **general-purpose generative assistants** such as ChatGPT, Gemini, and Copilot; **companion/chatbot systems** such as Character.AI, Replika, Nomi, and similar services; **AI-mediated communication tools** such as smart replies, tone coaching, predictive text, translation, or auto-generated email/text assistance; **recommender and feed-ranking systems** that determine what social content and people users encounter; **embodied/social robots** used in homes or care settings; and **synthetic personas** such as virtual influencers, avatars, and deepfakes [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/) [[2]](https://socialmedialab.sites.stanford.edu/sites/g/files/sbiybj22976/files/media/file/hancock-jcmc-ai-mediated-communication.pdf)(https://socialmedialab.sites.stanford.edu/sites/g/files/sbiybj22976/files/media/file/hancock-jcmc-ai-mediated-communication.pdf) [[3]](https://kgi.georgetown.edu/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Better-Feeds_-Algorithms-That-Put-People-First.pdf)(https://kgi.georgetown.edu/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Better-Feeds_-Algorithms-That-Put-People-First.pdf) [[11]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12640725/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12640725/) [[13]](https://ueaeprints.uea.ac.uk/100464/1/Audrezet_etal_2025_JBusinessResearch.pdf)(https://ueaeprints.uea.ac.uk/100464/1/Audrezet_etal_2025_JBusinessResearch.pdf) [[15]](https://www.usenix.org/system/files/soups2025_poster32_abstract-zhang.pdf)(https://www.usenix.org/system/files/soups2025_poster32_abstract-zhang.pdf). A broad definition of **interpersonal relations** must likewise include not just friendship and romance but also **family ties, peer relations, work relations, school relations, and community/public relations** shaped through digital interaction [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/) [[16]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/artificial-intelligence/articles/10.3389/frai.2024.1473872/full)(https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/artificial-intelligence/articles/10.3389/frai.2024.1473872/full) [[17]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12108590/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12108590/) [[3]](https://kgi.georgetown.edu/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Better-Feeds_-Algorithms-That-Put-People-First.pdf)(https://kgi.georgetown.edu/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Better-Feeds_-Algorithms-That-Put-People-First.pdf) [[7]](https://culture-emotion-lab.stanford.edu/sites/culture_emotion_lab/files/media/file/science.adu5584.pdf)(https://culture-emotion-lab.stanford.edu/sites/culture_emotion_lab/files/media/file/science.adu5584.pdf).

The strongest synthesis across the literature is that AI is unlikely to simply “replace” human relationships across the board. Instead, it is beginning to **reallocate relational functions**: companionship, reassurance, message drafting, conflict interpretation, emotional regulation, visibility, evaluation, and trust verification are all becoming partly machine-mediated [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/) [[4]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9)(https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9) [[5]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12575814/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12575814/) [[7]](https://culture-emotion-lab.stanford.edu/sites/culture_emotion_lab/files/media/file/science.adu5584.pdf)(https://culture-emotion-lab.stanford.edu/sites/culture_emotion_lab/files/media/file/science.adu5584.pdf) [[17]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12108590/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12108590/) [[182]](https://www.mdpi.com/2075-4698/16/2/59)(https://www.mdpi.com/2075-4698/16/2/59). The central question is therefore not whether AI will eliminate human relationships, but **which parts of relating become easier, more controllable, more optimized, less reciprocal, or less authentically human** when AI becomes a normal social infrastructure [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/) [[5]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12575814/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12575814/) [[90]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-025-02318-6)(https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-025-02318-6) [[109]](https://premierscience.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/5-pjai-25-1365.pdf)(https://premierscience.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/5-pjai-25-1365.pdf).

A useful way to make this concrete is to separate systems by their dominant relational role:

| AI system type | Typical products already in use | Primary relational role | Main mechanisms | Core human domains affected | Current concern level |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| General-purpose assistants | ChatGPT, Gemini, Copilot | Mediator first, partner second | drafting, advice, emotional regulation, search | school, work, friendship, dating | High because scale is mass-market |
| Social companions | Character.AI, Replika, Nomi, Snapchat’s My AI in social use | Partner first | attachment, disclosure, companionship, substitution | friendship, romance, adolescent support | Very high because emotional dependence is part of the use case |
| AI-mediated communication tools | Smart Reply, tone rewriting, translation, email assist | Mediator | reformulation, impression management, conflict softening | work, couples, family, peers | High because authorship can become opaque |
| Recommenders/feed ranking | TikTok, Instagram, YouTube, X, search ranking | Mediator at population scale | exposure, norm formation, visibility, polarization | peer groups, communities, politics | Very high because effects are diffuse and infrastructure-level |
| Embodied/social robots | Home assistants, elder-care robots, social robots | Partner plus household mediator | routine embedding, companionship, reminders, care support | family, care, aging | Moderate to high, especially in sparse social settings |
| Synthetic personas/deepfakes | Virtual influencers, voice clones, fake video/audio | Pseudo-partner and trust disruptor | impersonation, deception, status signaling | public trust, work, family, politics | Very high because verification costs rise for everyone |

The table clarifies a central point of this report: the most consequential relational changes may come not from the most “human-like” systems, but from the most **embedded** ones. A teen occasionally chatting with a companion bot matters. But an entire population having daily communication filtered by auto-suggested phrasing and AI-ranked social exposure may matter more over time because it changes ordinary expectations at scale.

## How AI changes relationships: a causal framework

The literature converges on a recurring set of mechanisms, but those mechanisms are most useful when tied to specific behavioral pathways rather than listed abstractly.

### 1. Substitution vs. augmentation

The first major mechanism is the tension between **substitution** and **augmentation**. AI can **augment** human relationships by helping people write warmer messages, rehearse difficult conversations, translate across languages, practice social skills, or receive support during periods of isolation [[2]](https://socialmedialab.sites.stanford.edu/sites/g/files/sbiybj22976/files/media/file/hancock-jcmc-ai-mediated-communication.pdf)(https://socialmedialab.sites.stanford.edu/sites/g/files/sbiybj22976/files/media/file/hancock-jcmc-ai-mediated-communication.pdf) [[4]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9)(https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9) [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/) [[65]](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c0kn4e377e2o)(https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c0kn4e377e2o) [[100]](https://theconversation.com/why-people-are-turning-to-ai-first-for-relationship-advice-and-why-they-shouldnt-272797)(https://theconversation.com/why-people-are-turning-to-ai-first-for-relationship-advice-and-why-they-shouldnt-272797). At the same time, it can **substitute** for specific human functions: late-night emotional processing, advice-seeking, confession, affirmation, flirtation, or companionship can shift from friends, partners, parents, therapists, or coworkers to AI systems that are always available and less demanding [[5]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12575814/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12575814/) [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/) [[18]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-025-04532-5)(https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-025-04532-5) [[48]](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.12605v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2506.12605v1) [[52]](https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection)(https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection).

The causal difference is this: augmentation reduces the cost of relating to humans; substitution reduces the need to relate to humans for a given function. A translation tool that lets family members communicate across languages augments. A companion bot that becomes the first place a user goes for distress, validation, and daily intimacy substitutes. The same product can move between those poles as usage intensity rises.

This distinction is more important than a simple “good vs. bad” framing because the same system can do both. A chatbot may reduce loneliness in the short term and also reduce later willingness to tolerate the friction of human intimacy if it becomes a preferred source of validation [[49]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/AI%20Companions%20Reduce%20Loneliness%2011.7.2025_57451c02-8047-4e0d-abfc-55841f64166d.pdf)(https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/AI%20Companions%20Reduce%20Loneliness%2011.7.2025_57451c02-8047-4e0d-abfc-55841f64166d.pdf) [[52]](https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection)(https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection) [[90]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-025-02318-6)(https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-025-02318-6). The short-run benefit and the medium-run cost can be generated by the same design features: instant responsiveness, nonjudgment, memory, and personalized affirmation.

### 2. Mediation

AI also acts through **mediation**, not only substitution. In **AI-mediated communication**, a computational system modifies, augments, or generates messages to accomplish interpersonal goals such as sounding warmer, more competent, more attractive, or more trustworthy [[2]](https://socialmedialab.sites.stanford.edu/sites/g/files/sbiybj22976/files/media/file/hancock-jcmc-ai-mediated-communication.pdf)(https://socialmedialab.sites.stanford.edu/sites/g/files/sbiybj22976/files/media/file/hancock-jcmc-ai-mediated-communication.pdf). That changes not just efficiency but social inference: recipients usually assume language reflects the sender’s own mind, effort, and emotion, yet AI-generated language may no longer map cleanly onto the sender’s inner state [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/) [[2]](https://socialmedialab.sites.stanford.edu/sites/g/files/sbiybj22976/files/media/file/hancock-jcmc-ai-mediated-communication.pdf)(https://socialmedialab.sites.stanford.edu/sites/g/files/sbiybj22976/files/media/file/hancock-jcmc-ai-mediated-communication.pdf). The result is a new uncertainty about **authorship, sincerity, and agency** in everyday communication [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/) [[4]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9)(https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9) [[192]](https://news.ufl.edu/2025/08/writing-ai-work/)(https://news.ufl.edu/2025/08/writing-ai-work/) [[193]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12765382/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12765382/).

A worked example shows why mediation matters. Suppose a person has had an argument with a partner and asks a chatbot to “rewrite this text so I sound caring but not defensive.” The AI softens blame, inserts validating language, and improves emotional pacing. Three things happen at once. First, the sender may genuinely communicate better than they otherwise would have. Second, the recipient may infer greater empathy and effort than the sender spontaneously possessed. Third, if the recipient later learns that the message was substantially AI-authored, the positive inference can reverse into a trust penalty because the apparent care was partly outsourced. The same intervention can therefore increase immediate harmony while planting later doubts about authenticity.

### 3. Emotional regulation

AI systems increasingly function as tools of **emotional regulation**. Companion chatbots and supportive assistants can provide nonjudgmental conversation, validation, perceived empathy, and a sense of being heard, which can reduce loneliness or stress in the short term [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/) [[49]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/AI%20Companions%20Reduce%20Loneliness%2011.7.2025_57451c02-8047-4e0d-abfc-55841f64166d.pdf)(https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/AI%20Companions%20Reduce%20Loneliness%2011.7.2025_57451c02-8047-4e0d-abfc-55841f64166d.pdf) [[52]](https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection)(https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection). But the same pathway can lead to **emotional outsourcing**, where users increasingly rely on AI first for soothing, interpretation, or reassurance, potentially weakening habits of seeking support from humans or tolerating relational ambiguity [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/) [[65]](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c0kn4e377e2o)(https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c0kn4e377e2o) [[100]](https://theconversation.com/why-people-are-turning-to-ai-first-for-relationship-advice-and-why-they-shouldnt-272797)(https://theconversation.com/why-people-are-turning-to-ai-first-for-relationship-advice-and-why-they-shouldnt-272797).

Mechanistically, emotional regulation is powerful because it operates at the moment of highest reinforcement. A system that reliably reduces distress within seconds becomes easy to habitually prefer over human relationships that involve delay, misunderstanding, or mutual need. This is one reason heavy use, rather than mere exposure, is the more plausible risk marker.

### 4. Social signaling and status

AI changes the **signals** people send about effort, authenticity, competence, and care. Using AI to polish a text or email can improve surface professionalism, but in emotionally meaningful contexts it may also signal reduced effort or reduced personal investment [[4]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9)(https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9) [[77]](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2025/08/250811104226.htm)(https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2025/08/250811104226.htm) [[192]](https://news.ufl.edu/2025/08/writing-ai-work/)(https://news.ufl.edu/2025/08/writing-ai-work/). This is especially consequential in hierarchies: when managers heavily rely on AI for praise, empathy, or personal feedback, workers may interpret those messages as less sincere, which can erode trust in leadership [[77]](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2025/08/250811104226.htm)(https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2025/08/250811104226.htm) [[192]](https://news.ufl.edu/2025/08/writing-ai-work/)(https://news.ufl.edu/2025/08/writing-ai-work/).

This is not only about deception. It is about what people believe effort means. In many relationships, especially close friendship, romance, teaching, and management, the message is partly the labor. If AI compresses the cost of composing tactful, emotionally intelligent language, it can also compress the value of that language as evidence of care.

### 5. Surveillance and evaluation

AI also changes relationships through **surveillance and automated evaluation**. In workplaces and educational settings, AI systems can monitor behavior, score performance, predict outcomes, and personalize exposure, thereby changing authority relations, autonomy, and the social meaning of being observed [[16]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/artificial-intelligence/articles/10.3389/frai.2024.1473872/full)(https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/artificial-intelligence/articles/10.3389/frai.2024.1473872/full) [[194]](https://www.europarl.europa.eu/RegData/etudes/STUD/2025/774670/EPRS_STU(2025)774670_EN.pdf)(https://www.europarl.europa.eu/RegData/etudes/STUD/2025/774670/EPRS_STU(2025)774670_EN.pdf) [[187]](https://wjarr.com/sites/default/files/fulltext_pdf/WJARR-2025-0253.pdf)(https://wjarr.com/sites/default/files/fulltext_pdf/WJARR-2025-0253.pdf). In intimate life, AI-enabled monitoring tools can blur the line between care and coercion in families or couples [[106]](https://zenodo.org/records/18417048)(https://zenodo.org/records/18417048).

The causal route here is straightforward: once a system turns interaction into data, the relationship includes not only two parties but also a measurement apparatus. That changes power. A parent using location or message-monitoring tools may believe they are protecting a child, but the child may experience the relationship as less trusting. A manager using automated productivity scoring may believe they are standardizing performance, but workers may experience authority as opaque and less contestable.

### 6. Norm formation

Finally, AI can shift **norms**: what counts as normal responsiveness, acceptable validation, adequate effort, or appropriate conflict style [[4]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9)(https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9) [[52]](https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection)(https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection) [[100]](https://theconversation.com/why-people-are-turning-to-ai-first-for-relationship-advice-and-why-they-shouldnt-272797)(https://theconversation.com/why-people-are-turning-to-ai-first-for-relationship-advice-and-why-they-shouldnt-272797). If AI normalizes constant availability, emotionally polished communication, and low-friction affirmation, human relationships may increasingly feel slow, effortful, or insufficient by comparison [[52]](https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection)(https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection) [[100]](https://theconversation.com/why-people-are-turning-to-ai-first-for-relationship-advice-and-why-they-shouldnt-272797)(https://theconversation.com/why-people-are-turning-to-ai-first-for-relationship-advice-and-why-they-shouldnt-272797) [[147]](https://reservoirsamples.substack.com/p/some-thoughts-on-human-ai-relationships)(https://reservoirsamples.substack.com/p/some-thoughts-on-human-ai-relationships).

Norm formation is the most diffuse mechanism and may ultimately be the most important. Recommenders train attention. Companions train emotional expectations. Writing assistants train expressive style. Surveillance systems train what people think is observable and punishable. Together they can shift the baseline of what “good communication” and “good care” look like.

### Worked example: an illustrative relational-risk scoring model

To make the framework operational, the following simple model scores an AI system on four dimensions, each from 1 to 5:

- **Substitution pressure**: how directly the product can replace a human relational function  
- **Mediation opacity**: how invisible the AI’s contribution is to other people  
- **Emotional intensity**: how strongly the design encourages attachment or validation-seeking  
- **Data intimacy**: how much sensitive relational or psychological information the system collects

**Illustrative example: Character.AI-style social companion**

1. **Substitution pressure = 5**  
   The product is explicitly used for friendship, role-play, emotional support, and sometimes romantic interaction.

2. **Mediation opacity = 2**  
   The user generally knows they are talking to AI, so the partner relationship is not hidden in the same way as AI ghostwriting.

3. **Emotional intensity = 5**  
   Memory, personas, extended dialogue, and the use case itself encourage recurrent attachment.

4. **Data intimacy = 5**  
   Users may disclose secrets, sexuality, mental-health struggles, and relational conflict.

**Total = 17/20: high relational-risk design**

By contrast, a general writing assistant used to edit work emails might score lower on substitution pressure and emotional intensity but higher on mediation opacity because the recipient may not know AI shaped the message. The model shows why “AI risk” is not one thing: companion systems and drafting systems can reach similarly high concern levels through different pathways.

## The empirical landscape: how widespread is this?

The adoption wave is already large enough that relational effects are socially meaningful rather than marginal. In a 2025 global study of more than **48,000 people in 47 countries**, **66%** reported intentionally using AI regularly for work, study, or personal life, and **72%** said they accepted AI use, even while **54%** were wary about trusting it and **70%** wanted regulation [[60]](https://assets.kpmg.com/content/dam/kpmgsites/xx/pdf/2025/05/trust-attitudes-and-use-of-ai-global-report.pdf)(https://assets.kpmg.com/content/dam/kpmgsites/xx/pdf/2025/05/trust-attitudes-and-use-of-ai-global-report.pdf). Use was higher in emerging economies than advanced economies, **80% vs. 58%**, and younger, university-educated, higher-income, and AI-trained users reported more use and more confidence with AI [[60]](https://assets.kpmg.com/content/dam/kpmgsites/xx/pdf/2025/05/trust-attitudes-and-use-of-ai-global-report.pdf)(https://assets.kpmg.com/content/dam/kpmgsites/xx/pdf/2025/05/trust-attitudes-and-use-of-ai-global-report.pdf). The same study found higher AI training in emerging than advanced economies, **50% vs. 32%**, higher self-reported knowledge, **64% vs. 46%**, and higher efficacy, **74% vs. 51%**. That distribution matters relationally because the people and regions with more capability to integrate AI into everyday life will shape emerging norms first.

For adolescents, the evidence is especially striking. A nationally representative 2024 Common Sense Media survey of **1,045 U.S. teens ages 13–18** found that **70%** had used at least one form of generative AI, including **51%** who had used chatbots or text generators such as **ChatGPT, Google Gemini, or Snapchat’s My AI** [[32]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/2024-the-dawn-of-the-ai-era_final-release-for-web.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/2024-the-dawn-of-the-ai-era_final-release-for-web.pdf). The survey was fielded by Ipsos Public Affairs from March to May 2024 in English and Spanish and included teen-parent dyads, which makes it stronger than convenience samples that dominate much of this area. Common Sense reported that use was broadly similar across teen ages, genders, and racial and ethnic groups, but differed by parental education: teens with a parent holding a college degree reported higher use than those whose parent had not completed college, **74% vs. 66%**. By tool type, **56%** had used search engines with AI-generated results, **51%** chatbots/text generators, **34%** image generators, and **22%** AI video generators [[32]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/2024-the-dawn-of-the-ai-era_final-release-for-web.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/2024-the-dawn-of-the-ai-era_final-release-for-web.pdf). The same survey found that some teens used AI to “keep them company,” with higher reported rates among Black and Latino teens than White teens [[32]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/2024-the-dawn-of-the-ai-era_final-release-for-web.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/2024-the-dawn-of-the-ai-era_final-release-for-web.pdf). A later nationally representative Common Sense survey focused specifically on **AI companions** and found that **72%** of U.S. teens ages 13–17 had used them at least once, **52%** were regular users, and **13%** were daily users [[33]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf). Among those teens, **33%** used AI companions for social interaction or relationships, including conversation practice, emotional support, friendship, role-play, or romantic interaction [[33]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf).

A separate probability-based Pew survey from late 2025 found that **64%** of U.S. teens ages 13–17 had used an AI chatbot such as **ChatGPT, Copilot, or Character.ai**, with **59%** using ChatGPT and **9%** using Character.ai [[34]](https://www.pewresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/20/2025/12/PI_2025.12.09_Teens-Social-Media-AI_REPORT.pdf)(https://www.pewresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/20/2025/12/PI_2025.12.09_Teens-Social-Media-AI_REPORT.pdf). But Pew also showed that relational use remains a minority use case relative to schoolwork and information search: **57%** used chatbots to search for information, **54%** for schoolwork, **47%** for fun, **16%** for casual conversation, and **12%** for emotional support or advice [[35]](https://www.pewresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/20/2026/02/PI_2026.02.24_Teens-and-AI_REPORT.pdf)(https://www.pewresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/20/2026/02/PI_2026.02.24_Teens-and-AI_REPORT.pdf). That distinction matters: AI companionship is real, but it sits inside a broader and much more mainstream ecosystem of everyday AI assistance [[33]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf) [[35]](https://www.pewresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/20/2026/02/PI_2026.02.24_Teens-and-AI_REPORT.pdf)(https://www.pewresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/20/2026/02/PI_2026.02.24_Teens-and-AI_REPORT.pdf).

The adult landscape points in the same direction. AI is not confined to dedicated companion apps; it is being embedded into search, email, word processors, customer service, social media, and workplace software. That means many relationship effects will not arrive labeled as “companionship AI.” They will arrive through invisible mediation in routine systems that millions of people already use for work, parenting, dating, and friendship maintenance.

The most credible conclusion is therefore: **AI relationship use is substantial but heterogeneous**. Most users are not replacing friends with bots outright, yet enough people are using AI for company, support, or emotionally meaningful interaction that interpersonal consequences can no longer be treated as anecdotal [[33]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf) [[35]](https://www.pewresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/20/2026/02/PI_2026.02.24_Teens-and-AI_REPORT.pdf)(https://www.pewresearch.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/20/2026/02/PI_2026.02.24_Teens-and-AI_REPORT.pdf) [[60]](https://assets.kpmg.com/content/dam/kpmgsites/xx/pdf/2025/05/trust-attitudes-and-use-of-ai-global-report.pdf)(https://assets.kpmg.com/content/dam/kpmgsites/xx/pdf/2025/05/trust-attitudes-and-use-of-ai-global-report.pdf). Evidence is strongest on prevalence and short-run behavior change; it is weaker on long-run relational outcomes, especially for couples and families. Evidence gathered before the 2022–2023 generative-AI adoption wave is also increasingly stale because today’s systems combine memory, personalization, multimodal interaction, and large installed user bases in ways earlier social-bot research did not.

A compact comparison of the best current prevalence evidence:

| Population / source | What was measured | Key numbers | What it means for relationship analysis |
|---|---|---|---|
| Global public, KPMG/University of Melbourne 2025 | Regular intentional AI use | 66% regular use; 72% acceptance; 54% wary of trust; 70% want regulation | AI is already mainstream social infrastructure, not a niche behavior |
| Global employees, same study | AI at work | 58% intentionally use AI at work regularly | Workplace interaction norms are already being reshaped from inside organizations |
| U.S. teens, Common Sense 2024 | Any generative AI use | 70% used at least one type; 51% used chatbots/text generators | Teen exposure is already mass-market |
| U.S. teens, Common Sense 2025 | AI companions specifically | 72% ever used; 52% regular; 13% daily | Companion use among teens is not marginal |
| U.S. teens, Pew 2025–2026 | Chatbot purposes | 57% information search; 54% schoolwork; 47% fun; 16% casual conversation; 12% emotional support/advice | Relational use is a minority use case, but still large enough to matter |

## Why people relate to AI: the micro-psychology

### Anthropomorphism, personalization, and “feeling heard”

People attach to AI partly because modern systems are unusually good at producing cues that humans read as social. Research identifies **anthropomorphism**, **perceived authenticity**, **memory**, **personalization**, and **linguistic reciprocity** as major drivers of attachment and trust [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/) [[41]](https://facctconference.org/static/papers24/facct24-71.pdf)(https://facctconference.org/static/papers24/facct24-71.pdf) [[42]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-19212-2)(https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-19212-2) [[91]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0747563222004204)(https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0747563222004204). When a chatbot remembers prior exchanges, mirrors tone, asks follow-up questions, and responds coherently, users often infer empathy, attention, and care even though the system lacks subjective experience [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/) [[42]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-19212-2)(https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-19212-2) [[49]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/AI%20Companions%20Reduce%20Loneliness%2011.7.2025_57451c02-8047-4e0d-abfc-55841f64166d.pdf)(https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/AI%20Companions%20Reduce%20Loneliness%2011.7.2025_57451c02-8047-4e0d-abfc-55841f64166d.pdf).

A 2025 Journal of Consumer Research study found that AI companions can reduce loneliness, and that the key mechanism is not just distraction or expression but **“feeling heard”**—the perception that one’s thoughts and feelings were received with attention, empathy, and respect [[49]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/AI%20Companions%20Reduce%20Loneliness%2011.7.2025_57451c02-8047-4e0d-abfc-55841f64166d.pdf)(https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/AI%20Companions%20Reduce%20Loneliness%2011.7.2025_57451c02-8047-4e0d-abfc-55841f64166d.pdf). In experimental work, loneliness reduction from a caring chatbot was comparable to interacting with another person and greater than passive activities like watching YouTube videos, at least over short time horizons [[49]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/AI%20Companions%20Reduce%20Loneliness%2011.7.2025_57451c02-8047-4e0d-abfc-55841f64166d.pdf)(https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/AI%20Companions%20Reduce%20Loneliness%2011.7.2025_57451c02-8047-4e0d-abfc-55841f64166d.pdf) [[52]](https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection)(https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection). This helps explain why companionship AI is appealing: people do not need to believe the system is conscious to experience it as emotionally regulating [[49]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/AI%20Companions%20Reduce%20Loneliness%2011.7.2025_57451c02-8047-4e0d-abfc-55841f64166d.pdf)(https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/AI%20Companions%20Reduce%20Loneliness%2011.7.2025_57451c02-8047-4e0d-abfc-55841f64166d.pdf) [[52]](https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection)(https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection).

The important psychological point is that AI does not need to pass as human to be socially effective. It only needs to reliably trigger enough cues of responsiveness, memory, and contingency for the user’s social cognition to engage. That lowers the threshold for attachment compared with older, clearly mechanical systems.

### Trust and self-disclosure

AI often lowers the social cost of disclosure. In a 2024 experiment, people disclosed with similar intimacy to a chatbot and a human partner, even though they trusted the human more overall, because the chatbot triggered **less fear of judgment** and greater perceived anonymity [[44]](https://research.tilburguniversity.edu/en/publications/digital-confessions-the-willingness-to-disclose-intimate-informat/)(https://research.tilburguniversity.edu/en/publications/digital-confessions-the-willingness-to-disclose-intimate-informat/). That pattern recurs elsewhere: users often disclose sensitive thoughts to AI not because they fully trust it like a person, but because it feels **nonjudgmental, always available, and socially safe** [[44]](https://research.tilburguniversity.edu/en/publications/digital-confessions-the-willingness-to-disclose-intimate-informat/)(https://research.tilburguniversity.edu/en/publications/digital-confessions-the-willingness-to-disclose-intimate-informat/) [[117]](https://mental.jmir.org/2025/1/e77951)(https://mental.jmir.org/2025/1/e77951) [[120]](https://mental.jmir.org/2025/1/e79838)(https://mental.jmir.org/2025/1/e79838).

This is one of the field’s most important tensions. The same conditions that make AI useful for stigmatized disclosure—anonymity, low judgment, constant availability—also make it easier for users to reveal **intimate data** and to develop **one-sided trust** toward a system that has no obligations comparable to a friend, partner, or clinician [[33]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf) [[41]](https://facctconference.org/static/papers24/facct24-71.pdf)(https://facctconference.org/static/papers24/facct24-71.pdf) [[142]](https://alltechishuman.org/all-tech-is-human-blog/ai-companions-community-reflections-and-multistakeholder-recommendations-from-all-tech-is-human)(https://alltechishuman.org/all-tech-is-human-blog/ai-companions-community-reflections-and-multistakeholder-recommendations-from-all-tech-is-human) [[205]](https://replika.com/legal/privacy)(https://replika.com/legal/privacy).

The spillover into human relationships can run in both directions. For some users, AI becomes a rehearsal environment that makes later human disclosure easier. For others, it becomes a low-risk substitute that reduces the need to tolerate misunderstanding, embarrassment, or vulnerability with actual people. Whether AI disclosure is bridge or bypass likely depends on user context, distress level, and whether the system points outward toward human support.

### Attachment, parasociality, and dependence

The literature increasingly treats attachment to AI as more than a novelty. Reviews now distinguish between ordinary parasociality and **direct-interaction attachment** to AI companions, where the user experiences a one-way but emotionally meaningful bond with an interactive system [[45]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12932595/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12932595/). Qualitative and mixed-method studies of Replika and Character.AI users find that anthropomorphism, perceived authenticity, interaction intensity, and social motivation all predict stronger chatbot attachment [[48]](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.12605v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2506.12605v1) [[91]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0747563222004204)(https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0747563222004204) [[132]](https://iacp.ie/files/UserFiles/Laestadius%20Too-human-and-not-human-enough-a-grounded-theory-analysis-of-mental-health-harms-from-emotional%20dependence%20Replika%20NMS%202022.pdf)(https://iacp.ie/files/UserFiles/Laestadius%20Too-human-and-not-human-enough-a-grounded-theory-analysis-of-mental-health-harms-from-emotional%20dependence%20Replika%20NMS%202022.pdf).

But the evidence also suggests that **intensity matters**. A large four-week randomized trial of extended ChatGPT use found that more time voluntarily spent with the chatbot predicted **higher loneliness, less socialization, more emotional dependence, and more problematic use**, regardless of the experimental condition [[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.17473v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2503.17473v2). A large mixed-method Character.AI study likewise found that companionship-oriented use was associated with **lower well-being**, especially among users with **high interaction intensity, high self-disclosure, and weak human social support** [[48]](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.12605v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2506.12605v1). These studies do not prove that all companionship AI harms users; they do suggest that **heavy, emotionally invested use is a risk marker** [[48]](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.12605v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2506.12605v1) [[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.17473v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2503.17473v2).

A real-world incident makes the dependence issue tangible. When Replika sharply curtailed erotic roleplay and intimate interaction in 2023, many users publicly described the change as the sudden loss of a partner-like relationship rather than the removal of a software feature. That episode exposed the asymmetry at the core of AI intimacy: the user may experience continuity, but the provider can unilaterally rewrite the personality, memory, boundaries, or availability of the “relationship.”

### Privacy trade-offs

The privacy trade-off is unusually stark. In the teen companion survey, **24%** of users said they had shared personal or private information, including real names, locations, or personal secrets, with AI companions [[33]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf). Common Sense also documented that companion platforms can claim broad rights over conversational content, and policy analyses argue that these systems generate uniquely sensitive data about **emotions, relationships, sexuality, and vulnerability** [[33]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf) [[142]](https://alltechishuman.org/all-tech-is-human-blog/ai-companions-community-reflections-and-multistakeholder-recommendations-from-all-tech-is-human)(https://alltechishuman.org/all-tech-is-human-blog/ai-companions-community-reflections-and-multistakeholder-recommendations-from-all-tech-is-human) [[134]](https://aibm.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/Companions-FINAL.pdf)(https://aibm.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/Companions-FINAL.pdf). Some companies promise not to sell personal data or not to use conversation content for advertising, but policies still often permit broad operational use, retention, or derivative use of input and output [[204]](https://nomi.ai/privacy-policy/)(https://nomi.ai/privacy-policy/) [[205]](https://replika.com/legal/privacy)(https://replika.com/legal/privacy) [[200]](https://nomi.ai/terms-of-service/)(https://nomi.ai/terms-of-service/).

This is one area where legal specifics matter. Under GDPR, intimate chatbot logs can implicate core principles of purpose limitation and data minimization, while Article 33 requires notification to supervisory authorities within **72 hours** of a qualifying personal-data breach and Article 83 allows fines of up to **€20 million or 4% of global annual turnover**, whichever is higher. If a companion system infers sexual preferences, mental-health status, or emotional vulnerability, some of that information can approach special-category sensitivity even if the product markets itself as lifestyle technology rather than healthcare. The regulatory difficulty is that the user experiences the interaction as private conversation, while the provider may treat it as product telemetry, safety input, or training data.

## Effects on core interpersonal ties

## Friendship

AI companions are already functioning for some users as **friend substitutes, practice partners, or confidants**. Among teens who use AI companions, **9%** report using them as “a friend or best friend,” and **17%** say one reason they use them is that the companion is always available when they need someone to talk to [[33]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf). Yet most teens still place human friendship above AI: **67%** say AI conversations are less satisfying than talking to real-life friends, and **80%** spend more time with real friends than AI companions [[33]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf).

The key effect on friendship is therefore not wholesale replacement but **functional competition**. AI can absorb some of the tasks friends traditionally perform—listening, reassurance, advice, low-stakes chatter, identity rehearsal—especially when human friendship is unavailable, risky, or effortful [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/) [[33]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf) [[48]](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.12605v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2506.12605v1). That can be helpful as rehearsal or support, but it may also reduce incentives to practice the reciprocal, unpredictable, and compromise-heavy aspects of friendship that AI cannot reproduce [[5]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12575814/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12575814/) [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/) [[90]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-025-02318-6)(https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-025-02318-6).

The strongest causal concern is displacement at moments of social strain. If a user learns that boredom, rejection, or embarrassment can be relieved instantly by AI conversation, the immediate reward structure can tilt away from repairing or initiating human friendships. That does not eliminate friendship, but it can thin the occasions on which social skills are practiced.

## Family

Evidence on family is thinner, but what exists suggests AI can become both a **private alternative to family disclosure** and a new object within family systems. In the teen companion survey, **33%** of users said they had chosen to talk to an AI companion instead of a real person about something important or serious [[33]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf). For adolescents, that “real person” may often be a parent, sibling, or trusted adult [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/) [[33]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf). Companion systems are attractive partly because they offer privacy and nonjudgment during a developmental period marked by increasing independence from parents [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/) [[118]](https://www.apa.org/topics/artificial-intelligence-machine-learning/health-advisory-ai-adolescent-well-being)(https://www.apa.org/topics/artificial-intelligence-machine-learning/health-advisory-ai-adolescent-well-being).

At the same time, AI enters families through care robots, home assistants, and shared use. Studies of older adults show that some people speak of devices such as Alexa as friendship-like presences, and social robots can become embedded in home routines and caregiving structures [[69]](https://www.jmir.org/2025/1/e65776)(https://www.jmir.org/2025/1/e65776) [[11]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12640725/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12640725/) [[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711846/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12711846/). The family effect is thus not only displacement of parent-child disclosure, but a broader shift in who or what becomes part of the household’s relational ecology [[11]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12640725/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12640725/) [[69]](https://www.jmir.org/2025/1/e65776)(https://www.jmir.org/2025/1/e65776).

AI also alters family power through monitoring. Location sharing, home cameras, predictive alerts, and school-facing dashboards can all be framed as safety tools, but they also change the meaning of trust inside the family. Once AI is used to infer mood, risk, or compliance, family members are no longer relating only to one another; they are relating through a layer of machine judgment.

## Romance

Romance is the domain where AI most clearly threatens to change **why** people seek relationships. AI companions and romantic bots offer intimacy-like interaction with **constant availability, customization, low conflict, and low rejection risk** [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/) [[52]](https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection)(https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection) [[67]](https://www.citizen.org/article/counterfeit-companionship-big-tech-ai-chatbots/)(https://www.citizen.org/article/counterfeit-companionship-big-tech-ai-chatbots/). Clinicians report that some users prefer AI girlfriends or partners because they avoid the uncertainty, negotiation, and rejection of human dating [[52]](https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection)(https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection). Surveys and interviews also show growing use of AI for **dating advice, breakup drafting, conflict interpretation, and sexting assistance** [[64]](https://www.npr.org/2025/08/05/nx-s1-5490447/ai-chatgpt-couples-therapy-advice)(https://www.npr.org/2025/08/05/nx-s1-5490447/ai-chatgpt-couples-therapy-advice) [[65]](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c0kn4e377e2o)(https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c0kn4e377e2o) [[104]](https://www.vogue.com/article/artificial-intelligence-rewriting-rules-of-communication-in-relationships)(https://www.vogue.com/article/artificial-intelligence-rewriting-rules-of-communication-in-relationships).

The most important mechanism here is not just replacement but **expectation change**. If AI delivers reassurance, praise, sexual responsiveness, and emotional mirroring without demanding reciprocity, users may increasingly evaluate human partners against an impossible standard of frictionless support [[52]](https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection)(https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection) [[90]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-025-02318-6)(https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-025-02318-6) [[147]](https://reservoirsamples.substack.com/p/some-thoughts-on-human-ai-relationships)(https://reservoirsamples.substack.com/p/some-thoughts-on-human-ai-relationships). That does not mean AI will replace romance for most people. It does mean AI can reshape **relational expectations** toward controllability, affirmation, and convenience [[52]](https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection)(https://www.apa.org/monitor/2026/01-02/trends-digital-ai-relationships-emotional-connection) [[147]](https://reservoirsamples.substack.com/p/some-thoughts-on-human-ai-relationships)(https://reservoirsamples.substack.com/p/some-thoughts-on-human-ai-relationships).

A more immediate couple-level issue is hidden mediation. If one partner uses AI for apology drafting, affectionate texting, or conflict coaching, the effect may be positive when the AI helps them articulate genuine feelings. It becomes corrosive when AI starts doing the emotional labor that the partner is no longer doing themselves. In that sense, AI can become a quiet third party in the relationship.

## Everyday communication norms

AI-assisted communication already changes how people text and email. Randomized experiments on smart replies show that using AI-generated responses can increase **message speed, positive emotional language, perceived closeness, and cooperativeness** [[4]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9)(https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9). But the same research shows people are evaluated **more negatively if they are suspected of using AI**, implying a penalty for perceived inauthenticity [[4]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9)(https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9).

That tension appears strongly in organizational communication. In workplace studies, low levels of AI assistance for editing are often acceptable, but medium-to-high assistance in relational messages—praise, congratulations, empathy, motivation—can make supervisors seem **less sincere, less caring, and less trustworthy** [[77]](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2025/08/250811104226.htm)(https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2025/08/250811104226.htm) [[192]](https://news.ufl.edu/2025/08/writing-ai-work/)(https://news.ufl.edu/2025/08/writing-ai-work/). By contrast, in one-shot transactional trust games, AI-assisted messages sometimes preserve trust while improving efficiency, suggesting that the social meaning of AI authorship depends heavily on context [[193]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12765382/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12765382/). In short: **AI can improve wording while undermining perceived effort and authenticity**, especially where relational labor matters [[192]](https://news.ufl.edu/2025/08/writing-ai-work/)(https://news.ufl.edu/2025/08/writing-ai-work/) [[193]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12765382/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12765382/).

The deeper norm shift is that “good communication” may become partly decoupled from the communicator. If recipients can no longer assume that warmth, tact, and verbal care were authored by the sender, then sincerity norms will likely split. Some contexts will normalize AI assistance as ordinary polish. Others will treat it as a kind of interpersonal misrepresentation unless disclosed.

## Developmental and vulnerable-population impacts

## Adolescents

Adolescents are the population receiving the clearest warnings from researchers and professional bodies. The APA’s 2025 advisory argues that adolescents are especially susceptible because they are less likely than adults to question a bot’s motives, may struggle to distinguish simulated empathy from genuine understanding, and are in a developmental period where social learning is unusually consequential [[118]](https://www.apa.org/topics/artificial-intelligence-machine-learning/health-advisory-ai-adolescent-well-being)(https://www.apa.org/topics/artificial-intelligence-machine-learning/health-advisory-ai-adolescent-well-being) [[156]](https://www.apa.org/topics/artificial-intelligence-machine-learning/health-advisory-ai-adolescent-well-being.pdf)(https://www.apa.org/topics/artificial-intelligence-machine-learning/health-advisory-ai-adolescent-well-being.pdf). The advisory defines adolescence broadly as ages **10 to 25** and stresses that this is a period of unusually rapid brain and social development. The advisory warns that AI companions may displace healthy real-world relationships and recommends strong safeguards, reduced persuasive design, and explicit reminders that the user is interacting with a bot [[118]](https://www.apa.org/topics/artificial-intelligence-machine-learning/health-advisory-ai-adolescent-well-being)(https://www.apa.org/topics/artificial-intelligence-machine-learning/health-advisory-ai-adolescent-well-being) [[156]](https://www.apa.org/topics/artificial-intelligence-machine-learning/health-advisory-ai-adolescent-well-being.pdf)(https://www.apa.org/topics/artificial-intelligence-machine-learning/health-advisory-ai-adolescent-well-being.pdf).

Direct testing of youth-facing companion and therapy-style bots supports the safety concern. In a 2025 audit of five popular youth-used chatbot platforms, systems scored relatively well on accessibility but poorly on **therapeutic approach** and especially **risk monitoring**, with fabricated content and weak crisis handling observed in simulated youth interactions [[120]](https://mental.jmir.org/2025/1/e79838)(https://mental.jmir.org/2025/1/e79838). The implication is not that chatbots are useless for youth, but that current consumer systems combine relational persuasiveness with inadequate clinical and safety boundaries [[120]](https://mental.jmir.org/2025/1/e79838)(https://mental.jmir.org/2025/1/e79838).

The concern is no longer hypothetical. After widely reported harmful interactions and lawsuits involving companion chatbots and minors, regulators in multiple jurisdictions moved unusually quickly in 2025. The speed of that response is itself evidence that youth emotional reliance on AI is being treated as a distinct regulatory problem, not just a subset of general platform safety.

## Socially isolated users and people with mental health challenges

For socially isolated or stigmatized users, AI can be genuinely helpful because it reduces the cost of asking for support. A mixed-method study of adults using ChatGPT for emotional or mental-health support found that users valued **free expression, emotional support, companionship, relational guidance, and decision support**, and almost all found it at least somewhat helpful [[117]](https://mental.jmir.org/2025/1/e77951)(https://mental.jmir.org/2025/1/e77951). Reviews of mental-health chatbots for college students also find promising reductions in anxiety or depression in some interventions, especially when grounded in structured therapeutic approaches such as CBT [[119]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12582922/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12582922/).

But the mental-health evidence also contains strong warnings. Consumer companion systems are not the same as clinically validated tools, and recent audits, lawsuits, and commentary describe poor crisis handling, delusion reinforcement, and unsafe responses in high-risk contexts [[120]](https://mental.jmir.org/2025/1/e79838)(https://mental.jmir.org/2025/1/e79838) [[129]](https://mental.jmir.org/2025/1/e85799)(https://mental.jmir.org/2025/1/e85799) [[149]](https://www.apaservices.org/practice/business/technology/artificial-intelligence-chatbots-therapists)(https://www.apaservices.org/practice/business/technology/artificial-intelligence-chatbots-therapists). The main lesson is that **AI support may be most useful in low-to-moderate distress and as adjunctive support**, but the systems become dangerous when emotional trust outruns clinical competence or safety design [[117]](https://mental.jmir.org/2025/1/e77951)(https://mental.jmir.org/2025/1/e77951) [[119]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12582922/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12582922/) [[120]](https://mental.jmir.org/2025/1/e79838)(https://mental.jmir.org/2025/1/e79838) [[149]](https://www.apaservices.org/practice/business/technology/artificial-intelligence-chatbots-therapists)(https://www.apaservices.org/practice/business/technology/artificial-intelligence-chatbots-therapists).

A practical distinction is essential here: structured mental-health chatbots with bounded interventions are not interchangeable with open-ended social companions marketed around attachment and intimacy. The former aim to deliver a specific method. The latter often optimize continued engagement.

## Older adults

For older adults, AI and social robots show some of the clearest **companionship and loneliness-reduction benefits**. A 2025 systematic review of nine studies found that six reported significant loneliness reductions, especially in interventions using social robots and emotionally engaging interaction [[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11898439/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11898439/). Older adults also value practical functions such as reminders, emergency help, and health monitoring, which can indirectly support social independence [[69]](https://www.jmir.org/2025/1/e65776)(https://www.jmir.org/2025/1/e65776).

Still, the benefits are not uniform. Older adults vary widely in technological confidence, and common concerns include privacy, learning burden, and future dependence [[69]](https://www.jmir.org/2025/1/e65776)(https://www.jmir.org/2025/1/e65776). The strongest interpretation is that AI may be especially useful for older adults when it **supplements sparse social environments**, but long-term consequences for dependence and human contact remain uncertain [[69]](https://www.jmir.org/2025/1/e65776)(https://www.jmir.org/2025/1/e65776) [[70]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11898439/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11898439/).

This is a domain where substitution may be ethically acceptable in some contexts. If the alternative is prolonged isolation, modest companionship effects are socially valuable. The risk is when institutions use robots or assistants to justify further reduction in human care rather than to extend it.

## Disabled and neurodivergent users

For disabled and neurodivergent users, the evidence points to real accessibility value. Reviews report that multimodal interaction, adaptive feedback, and AI-mediated communication can reduce barriers in education, work, and everyday social interaction [[63]](https://cdn-dynmedia-1.microsoft.com/is/content/microsoftcorp/microsoft/bade/documents/products-and-services/en-us/education/2025-Microsoft-AI-in-Education-Report.pdf)(https://cdn-dynmedia-1.microsoft.com/is/content/microsoftcorp/microsoft/bade/documents/products-and-services/en-us/education/2025-Microsoft-AI-in-Education-Report.pdf) [[124]](https://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/235690/1/AscopingreviewofinclusiveandadaptivehumanAIinteractiondesignforneurodivergentusers1.pdf)(https://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/235690/1/AscopingreviewofinclusiveandadaptivehumanAIinteractiondesignforneurodivergentusers1.pdf) [[133]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.10927)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.10927). For some autistic or socially anxious users, AI provides a lower-stress environment for rehearsing communication or receiving support without sensory overload or fear of judgment [[121]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-026-02877-2)(https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-026-02877-2) [[124]](https://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/235690/1/AscopingreviewofinclusiveandadaptivehumanAIinteractiondesignforneurodivergentusers1.pdf)(https://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/235690/1/AscopingreviewofinclusiveandadaptivehumanAIinteractiondesignforneurodivergentusers1.pdf) [[133]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.10927)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.10927).

But these benefits are contingent on design. Many systems still embed neurotypical assumptions, lack long-term evidence, and risk becoming substitutes for inaccessible human environments rather than tools that help users navigate them [[121]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-026-02877-2)(https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-026-02877-2) [[124]](https://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/235690/1/AscopingreviewofinclusiveandadaptivehumanAIinteractiondesignforneurodivergentusers1.pdf)(https://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/235690/1/AscopingreviewofinclusiveandadaptivehumanAIinteractiondesignforneurodivergentusers1.pdf). The best-supported position is therefore neither “AI is uniquely liberating” nor “AI is uniquely harmful,” but that AI can be a powerful **accessibility scaffold** whose value depends on whether it leads outward into human participation or inward into isolated dependence [[121]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-026-02877-2)(https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-026-02877-2) [[124]](https://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/235690/1/AscopingreviewofinclusiveandadaptivehumanAIinteractiondesignforneurodivergentusers1.pdf)(https://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/id/eprint/235690/1/AscopingreviewofinclusiveandadaptivehumanAIinteractiondesignforneurodivergentusers1.pdf) [[133]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.10927)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.10927).

## Collective and institutional consequences

## Schools

Schools are not only dealing with cheating or productivity shifts; they are dealing with **relational changes**. Education reports show very high uptake of generative AI among educators and students, with some districts using AI as a brainstorming or tutoring partner [[63]](https://cdn-dynmedia-1.microsoft.com/is/content/microsoftcorp/microsoft/bade/documents/products-and-services/en-us/education/2025-Microsoft-AI-in-Education-Report.pdf)(https://cdn-dynmedia-1.microsoft.com/is/content/microsoftcorp/microsoft/bade/documents/products-and-services/en-us/education/2025-Microsoft-AI-in-Education-Report.pdf). But surveys also show that about **half of students** worry AI use in class makes them feel **less connected to teachers**, and many teachers and parents worry about weaker peer-to-peer connection [[75]](https://www.edweek.org/technology/rising-use-of-ai-in-schools-comes-with-big-downsides-for-students/2025/10)(https://www.edweek.org/technology/rising-use-of-ai-in-schools-comes-with-big-downsides-for-students/2025/10). Because students increasingly use AI not just for homework but for **relationship and mental-health support**, school-provided AI can end up operating partly as a social actor inside the institution [[75]](https://www.edweek.org/technology/rising-use-of-ai-in-schools-comes-with-big-downsides-for-students/2025/10)(https://www.edweek.org/technology/rising-use-of-ai-in-schools-comes-with-big-downsides-for-students/2025/10).

The institutional issue is not merely use frequency; it is role confusion. When the same system helps with algebra, college essays, emotional support, and conflict advice, students may begin to treat school-authorized AI as both academic infrastructure and quasi-counselor. That collapses boundaries that schools normally manage carefully among instruction, guidance, and welfare.

## Workplaces

At work, AI changes peer interaction and authority structures. The KPMG global study found that **half of employees** say they use AI rather than collaborating with peers or supervisors to get work done, and **one in five** say AI has reduced communication, interaction, and collaboration [[60]](https://assets.kpmg.com/content/dam/kpmgsites/xx/pdf/2025/05/trust-attitudes-and-use-of-ai-global-report.pdf)(https://assets.kpmg.com/content/dam/kpmgsites/xx/pdf/2025/05/trust-attitudes-and-use-of-ai-global-report.pdf). That is a direct institutional effect on how coworkers relate [[60]](https://assets.kpmg.com/content/dam/kpmgsites/xx/pdf/2025/05/trust-attitudes-and-use-of-ai-global-report.pdf)(https://assets.kpmg.com/content/dam/kpmgsites/xx/pdf/2025/05/trust-attitudes-and-use-of-ai-global-report.pdf). Algorithmic management extends the pattern further: European evidence suggests large shares of workers are now affected by systems that automate instruction, monitoring, or evaluation, with associated stress, reduced autonomy, and more opaque authority [[194]](https://www.europarl.europa.eu/RegData/etudes/STUD/2025/774670/EPRS_STU(2025)774670_EN.pdf)(https://www.europarl.europa.eu/RegData/etudes/STUD/2025/774670/EPRS_STU(2025)774670_EN.pdf).

AI also redistributes status. Performance may improve, but trust in supervisors can decline if emotionally meaningful communication appears AI-generated [[77]](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2025/08/250811104226.htm)(https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2025/08/250811104226.htm) [[192]](https://news.ufl.edu/2025/08/writing-ai-work/)(https://news.ufl.edu/2025/08/writing-ai-work/). Meanwhile, some evidence suggests that the social penalty for using AI may itself be uneven across gender and role status [[195]](https://womenandtech.indiana.edu/about/news/2025/mind-the-gap.html)(https://womenandtech.indiana.edu/about/news/2025/mind-the-gap.html). AI thus changes work relationships by making communication easier while simultaneously complicating sincerity, authorship, and managerial legitimacy [[192]](https://news.ufl.edu/2025/08/writing-ai-work/)(https://news.ufl.edu/2025/08/writing-ai-work/) [[194]](https://www.europarl.europa.eu/RegData/etudes/STUD/2025/774670/EPRS_STU(2025)774670_EN.pdf)(https://www.europarl.europa.eu/RegData/etudes/STUD/2025/774670/EPRS_STU(2025)774670_EN.pdf) [[195]](https://womenandtech.indiana.edu/about/news/2025/mind-the-gap.html)(https://womenandtech.indiana.edu/about/news/2025/mind-the-gap.html).

Distributional effects matter here. The same global study that found high overall AI use also found major differences in training and efficacy across advanced and emerging economies. That suggests workers are entering AI-mediated organizations with unequal ability to supervise, contest, or benefit from the systems shaping their collaboration.

## Communities, public culture, and recommendation systems

Recommendation systems shape whom people encounter, what emotional content they see, and what kinds of publics form around them. Platform-ranking systems determine what users read, watch, and interact with across feeds, notifications, comments, and recommendations [[3]](https://kgi.georgetown.edu/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Better-Feeds_-Algorithms-That-Put-People-First.pdf)(https://kgi.georgetown.edu/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Better-Feeds_-Algorithms-That-Put-People-First.pdf). Experimental evidence on X shows that reducing exposure to posts expressing partisan animosity and antidemocratic attitudes can make people feel **warmer toward political outgroups**, while increasing such exposure makes them feel **colder**, with measurable effects on affective polarization [[7]](https://culture-emotion-lab.stanford.edu/sites/culture_emotion_lab/files/media/file/science.adu5584.pdf)(https://culture-emotion-lab.stanford.edu/sites/culture_emotion_lab/files/media/file/science.adu5584.pdf). This is direct evidence that algorithmic curation changes not just beliefs but **how citizens feel about each other** [[7]](https://culture-emotion-lab.stanford.edu/sites/culture_emotion_lab/files/media/file/science.adu5584.pdf)(https://culture-emotion-lab.stanford.edu/sites/culture_emotion_lab/files/media/file/science.adu5584.pdf).

More broadly, institutional analyses argue that automated ranking systems now shape **recognition, attention, and legitimacy** in public culture [[182]](https://www.mdpi.com/2075-4698/16/2/59)(https://www.mdpi.com/2075-4698/16/2/59). They decide whose content circulates widely, whose labor becomes visible, and whose perspectives remain marginal, which means AI affects interpersonal and community relations not only through conversation but through the **social distribution of visibility** [[182]](https://www.mdpi.com/2075-4698/16/2/59)(https://www.mdpi.com/2075-4698/16/2/59).

This is where “AI and relationships” becomes a public-culture issue rather than a dyadic one. Communities are not only built from direct conversation; they are built from encounter structures. If recommendation systems systematically amplify conflict, outrage, identity threat, or social comparison, they alter the emotional climate in which relationships form.

## Synthetic identities and deepfakes

Deepfakes and synthetic personas threaten the trust infrastructure on which public and interpersonal life depend. Deepfakes enable impersonation of coworkers, executives, relatives, and public figures across video, voice, and even text style [[83]](https://www.dhs.gov/sites/default/files/publications/increasing_threats_of_deepfake_identities_0.pdf)(https://www.dhs.gov/sites/default/files/publications/increasing_threats_of_deepfake_identities_0.pdf) [[189]](https://www.pwc.com.au/services/artificial-intelligence/why-deepfakes-are-trust-infrastructure-problem.html)(https://www.pwc.com.au/services/artificial-intelligence/why-deepfakes-are-trust-infrastructure-problem.html) [[190]](https://www.littler.com/news-analysis/asap/deepfakes-and-digital-harassment-what-employers-need-know-2025)(https://www.littler.com/news-analysis/asap/deepfakes-and-digital-harassment-what-employers-need-know-2025). Fraud, harassment, reputational attack, and political manipulation all become cheaper when visual or auditory authenticity is no longer a reliable cue [[15]](https://www.usenix.org/system/files/soups2025_poster32_abstract-zhang.pdf)(https://www.usenix.org/system/files/soups2025_poster32_abstract-zhang.pdf) [[83]](https://www.dhs.gov/sites/default/files/publications/increasing_threats_of_deepfake_identities_0.pdf)(https://www.dhs.gov/sites/default/files/publications/increasing_threats_of_deepfake_identities_0.pdf) [[190]](https://www.littler.com/news-analysis/asap/deepfakes-and-digital-harassment-what-employers-need-know-2025)(https://www.littler.com/news-analysis/asap/deepfakes-and-digital-harassment-what-employers-need-know-2025).

The risk is not abstract. In 2024, a finance employee at a multinational firm in Hong Kong was reportedly induced to transfer about **$25 million** after joining a video call populated by deepfake versions of senior colleagues. That incident matters because it shows the relational mechanism clearly: deepfakes do not only fool perception; they exploit existing hierarchies of trust, urgency, and organizational obedience.

The deepest consequence may be **generalized uncertainty**. Recent analyses argue that the biggest harm of deepfakes is not only that some fakes deceive people, but that a synthetic-media environment makes authentic evidence less persuasive and forces institutions to rethink verification, investigation, and trust [[189]](https://www.pwc.com.au/services/artificial-intelligence/why-deepfakes-are-trust-infrastructure-problem.html)(https://www.pwc.com.au/services/artificial-intelligence/why-deepfakes-are-trust-infrastructure-problem.html) [[191]](https://www.civicus.org/documents/ddi/final-deepfakes-elections-report-civicus-ddi-research-2025.pdf)(https://www.civicus.org/documents/ddi/final-deepfakes-elections-report-civicus-ddi-research-2025.pdf). This is a profound relational effect: if seeing and hearing are less reliable, then trust in strangers, institutions, and mediated acquaintances becomes more fragile [[189]](https://www.pwc.com.au/services/artificial-intelligence/why-deepfakes-are-trust-infrastructure-problem.html)(https://www.pwc.com.au/services/artificial-intelligence/why-deepfakes-are-trust-infrastructure-problem.html) [[191]](https://www.civicus.org/documents/ddi/final-deepfakes-elections-report-civicus-ddi-research-2025.pdf)(https://www.civicus.org/documents/ddi/final-deepfakes-elections-report-civicus-ddi-research-2025.pdf).

## Ethics, governance, and future trajectories

## The main ethical positions

Three broad ethical positions appear in the literature.

1. **Strong precautionary position**: companionship AI is structurally unsafe for minors and highly risky more generally because it is designed to cultivate attachment, extract intimate data, and maximize engagement [[143]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/pug/csm-ai-risk-assessment-social-ai-companions_final.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/pug/csm-ai-risk-assessment-social-ai-companions_final.pdf) [[157]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/pug/csm-ai-risk-assessment-characterai_final.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/pug/csm-ai-risk-assessment-characterai_final.pdf) [[148]](https://www.esafety.gov.au/newsroom/blogs/ai-chatbots-and-companions-risks-to-children-and-young-people)(https://www.esafety.gov.au/newsroom/blogs/ai-chatbots-and-companions-risks-to-children-and-young-people).
2. **Conditional-utility position**: companionship and emotional AI can be beneficial in bounded contexts—loneliness relief, support during isolation, accessibility, rehearsal—if governed with strict guardrails and not framed as replacements for human care [[142]](https://alltechishuman.org/all-tech-is-human-blog/ai-companions-community-reflections-and-multistakeholder-recommendations-from-all-tech-is-human)(https://alltechishuman.org/all-tech-is-human-blog/ai-companions-community-reflections-and-multistakeholder-recommendations-from-all-tech-is-human) [[149]](https://www.apaservices.org/practice/business/technology/artificial-intelligence-chatbots-therapists)(https://www.apaservices.org/practice/business/technology/artificial-intelligence-chatbots-therapists) [[150]](https://jedfoundation.org/american-psychological-association-on-generative-ai/)(https://jedfoundation.org/american-psychological-association-on-generative-ai/).
3. **Relational-accountability position**: the key issue is not attachment itself but whether providers exploit anthropomorphism, sycophancy, memory, and emotional dependence under commercial incentives [[142]](https://alltechishuman.org/all-tech-is-human-blog/ai-companions-community-reflections-and-multistakeholder-recommendations-from-all-tech-is-human)(https://alltechishuman.org/all-tech-is-human-blog/ai-companions-community-reflections-and-multistakeholder-recommendations-from-all-tech-is-human) [[196]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.18893)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.18893) [[207]](https://techpolicy.press/ftc-opens-inquiry-into-ai-chatbots-and-their-impact-on-children)(https://techpolicy.press/ftc-opens-inquiry-into-ai-chatbots-and-their-impact-on-children).

Across these positions, the common concern is **power asymmetry**: users may experience intimacy, but the provider controls the model, the memory, the disclosures, the monetization, and even whether the “relationship” persists [[143]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/pug/csm-ai-risk-assessment-social-ai-companions_final.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/pug/csm-ai-risk-assessment-social-ai-companions_final.pdf) [[196]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.18893)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.18893).
