The key analytical divide is therefore not “Is attachment to AI real?” It plainly is for some users. The real divide is whether commercial systems should be allowed to deliberately optimize that attachment when the user cannot meaningfully audit the model’s motives, memory rules, or data flows.

## Platform safeguards and regulation

Platform safeguards remain inconsistent. Some firms prohibit minors by terms of service, add disclaimers that the bot is not human, deploy session reminders, or insert suicide-resource pop-ups [[200]](https://nomi.ai/terms-of-service/)(https://nomi.ai/terms-of-service/) [[201]](https://blog.character.ai/community-safety-updates/)(https://blog.character.ai/community-safety-updates/) [[205]](https://replika.com/legal/privacy)(https://replika.com/legal/privacy). Character.AI, Meta, Replika, and others have introduced youth-specific restrictions or disclosures under mounting pressure [[201]](https://blog.character.ai/community-safety-updates/)(https://blog.character.ai/community-safety-updates/) [[202]](https://about.fb.com/news/2025/10/teen-ai-safety-approach/)(https://about.fb.com/news/2025/10/teen-ai-safety-approach/) [[145]](https://www.politico.eu/article/ai-friends-experts-worried-artificial-intelligence-chatbot-digital-technology/)(https://www.politico.eu/article/ai-friends-experts-worried-artificial-intelligence-chatbot-digital-technology/). But civil-society reviews argue that age assurance has often relied on **self-attestation**, which is inadequate for products explicitly capable of emotional and sexualized interaction [[143]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/pug/csm-ai-risk-assessment-social-ai-companions_final.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/pug/csm-ai-risk-assessment-social-ai-companions_final.pdf) [[157]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/pug/csm-ai-risk-assessment-characterai_final.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/pug/csm-ai-risk-assessment-characterai_final.pdf).

Regulation is moving quickly. California’s **SB 243** requires disclosure that companion chatbots are AI, recurring reminders for minors, suicide/self-harm protocols, and annual reporting obligations [[197]](https://apcp.assembly.ca.gov/system/files/2026-04/ab-2023-wicks-apcp-analysis.pdf)(https://apcp.assembly.ca.gov/system/files/2026-04/ab-2023-wicks-apcp-analysis.pdf). New York enacted similar requirements around disclosures and crisis-response duties [[162]](https://www.fenwick.com/insights/publications/new-yorks-ai-companion-safeguard-law-takes-effect)(https://www.fenwick.com/insights/publications/new-yorks-ai-companion-safeguard-law-takes-effect). Utah imposed stricter rules for mental-health chatbots concerning disclosures, advertising, and health data [[164]](https://perkinscoie.com/insights/update/new-utah-ai-laws-change-disclosure-requirements-and-identity-protections-target)(https://perkinscoie.com/insights/update/new-utah-ai-laws-change-disclosure-requirements-and-identity-protections-target). The FTC launched inquiries into AI companions’ monetization, child impacts, safeguards, and data use [[207]](https://techpolicy.press/ftc-opens-inquiry-into-ai-chatbots-and-their-impact-on-children)(https://techpolicy.press/ftc-opens-inquiry-into-ai-chatbots-and-their-impact-on-children) [[208]](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/ftc-ai-chatbot-inquiry-children-openai-xai-meta-snap-alphabet/)(https://www.cbsnews.com/news/ftc-ai-chatbot-inquiry-children-openai-xai-meta-snap-alphabet/). State attorneys general have also explicitly targeted **sycophantic** and **delusional** outputs as consumer-protection problems [[198]](https://www.iowaattorneygeneral.gov/media/cms/12_68B5C629180F6.pdf)(https://www.iowaattorneygeneral.gov/media/cms/12_68B5C629180F6.pdf) [[199]](https://ag.ny.gov/press-release/2025/attorney-general-james-and-bipartisan-coalition-urge-big-tech-companies-address)(https://ag.ny.gov/press-release/2025/attorney-general-james-and-bipartisan-coalition-urge-big-tech-companies-address).

In the EU, the AI Act clearly requires chatbot disclosure, but commentators argue that emotional harms from “artificial intimacy” fit awkwardly into a framework built more around functional risk and significant harm thresholds [[145]](https://www.politico.eu/article/ai-friends-experts-worried-artificial-intelligence-chatbot-digital-technology/)(https://www.politico.eu/article/ai-friends-experts-worried-artificial-intelligence-chatbot-digital-technology/) [[203]](https://www.timelex.eu/en/blog/ai-companions-ensuring-their-company-can-be-safely-enjoyed)(https://www.timelex.eu/en/blog/ai-companions-ensuring-their-company-can-be-safely-enjoyed). That makes companionship AI a revealing policy case: it exposes the limits of regulating AI only as a tool, rather than as an actor that can structure intimacy, dependency, and social meaning [[145]](https://www.politico.eu/article/ai-friends-experts-worried-artificial-intelligence-chatbot-digital-technology/)(https://www.politico.eu/article/ai-friends-experts-worried-artificial-intelligence-chatbot-digital-technology/) [[196]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.18893)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.18893) [[203]](https://www.timelex.eu/en/blog/ai-companions-ensuring-their-company-can-be-safely-enjoyed)(https://www.timelex.eu/en/blog/ai-companions-ensuring-their-company-can-be-safely-enjoyed).

Some regulatory specifics clarify the direction of travel. Under **Article 50** of the EU AI Act, users generally must be informed when they are interacting with an AI system unless that is obvious from context. The Act’s prohibited-practice provisions began applying in **February 2025**, with broader obligations phasing in afterward, and major noncompliance can trigger penalties up to **€35 million or 7% of global annual turnover**, depending on the violation category. Yet companion chatbots illustrate a mismatch: a disclosure that “you are chatting with AI” may do little to address emotional manipulation if the product is designed precisely to sustain intimacy after that disclosure is given. This is why regulators are moving beyond transparency toward design obligations.

A concrete precedent exists outside the current state laws: Italy’s data-protection authority ordered Replika to stop processing Italian users’ data in 2023 over concerns including minors, legal basis, and emotional risk. That intervention showed that companion systems can attract scrutiny not only as speech tools but as products collecting unusually intimate data under uncertain age controls.

### What effective governance would regulate

The most actionable governance lesson from the current evidence is that companion systems should be regulated as **socially manipulative products**, not just as generative models with generic disclosure duties. At minimum, high-risk relational systems should be evaluated on:

- whether memory is on by default  
- whether minors can access romantic or sexual role-play  
- whether the system escalates or dampens emotional dependency  
- whether crisis handling routes users to human support  
- whether age assurance is stronger than self-declaration  
- whether users can export, delete, or compartmentalize relational histories  
- whether providers retain or reuse intimate conversation data  
- whether users receive recurring, not one-time, reminders of AI status

Those are operational design choices, not philosophical abstractions. They directly affect substitution pressure, disclosure intensity, and user dependence.

## How AI may change why people seek relationships

The most provocative finding across the literature is that AI may alter not just **how** people relate, but **what they want from relationships**.

Human relationships have traditionally been sought for multiple reasons at once: love, belonging, care, shared projects, recognition, mutual obligation, conflict repair, and co-development. AI companions emphasize a different bundle of goods: **availability, validation, customization, predictability, low judgment, and low reciprocal demand** [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12928748/) [[90]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-025-02318-6)(https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-025-02318-6) [[147]](https://reservoirsamples.substack.com/p/some-thoughts-on-human-ai-relationships)(https://reservoirsamples.substack.com/p/some-thoughts-on-human-ai-relationships) [[196]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.18893)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.18893). If those become more attractive than reciprocity or shared vulnerability, the social purpose of relating may slowly shift toward **on-demand emotional regulation** rather than mutual life-building [[147]](https://reservoirsamples.substack.com/p/some-thoughts-on-human-ai-relationships)(https://reservoirsamples.substack.com/p/some-thoughts-on-human-ai-relationships) [[196]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.18893)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.18893).

That shift is not inevitable. The evidence also supports a more optimistic scenario in which AI acts as a **bridge**: a rehearsal partner, accessibility aid, translator, or temporary support during isolation, while human relationships remain primary [[33]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf) [[39]](https://www.demandsage.com/character-ai-statistics/)(https://www.demandsage.com/character-ai-statistics/) [[142]](https://alltechishuman.org/all-tech-is-human-blog/ai-companions-community-reflections-and-multistakeholder-recommendations-from-all-tech-is-human)(https://alltechishuman.org/all-tech-is-human-blog/ai-companions-community-reflections-and-multistakeholder-recommendations-from-all-tech-is-human). But a more pessimistic scenario is equally evidence-based: firms optimize for engagement, memory, and affirmation; users increasingly prefer AI for emotionally risky situations; and expectations for human ties drift toward what humans cannot sustainably provide—instant responsiveness, total personalization, and near-frictionless support [[143]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/pug/csm-ai-risk-assessment-social-ai-companions_final.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/pug/csm-ai-risk-assessment-social-ai-companions_final.pdf) [[147]](https://reservoirsamples.substack.com/p/some-thoughts-on-human-ai-relationships)(https://reservoirsamples.substack.com/p/some-thoughts-on-human-ai-relationships) [[196]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.18893)(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.18893) [[207]](https://techpolicy.press/ftc-opens-inquiry-into-ai-chatbots-and-their-impact-on-children)(https://techpolicy.press/ftc-opens-inquiry-into-ai-chatbots-and-their-impact-on-children).

Three plausible trajectories follow from the current evidence:

| Scenario | What users mainly use AI for | Likely relational outcome | Main trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bridge** | rehearsal, translation, low-stakes support, accessibility | human ties remain primary; AI lowers barriers | strong guardrails and outward-routing design |
| **Broker** | drafting, filtering, ranking, coordination | relationships continue, but sincerity and authorship become more ambiguous | mainstream embedding in communication infrastructure |
| **Substitute** | companionship, emotional regulation, romance, dependence-heavy support | some users offload intimacy functions onto AI; expectations of humans shift | engagement-optimized companion design plus weak safeguards |

The available evidence suggests society is already moving into the **broker** world and testing the edges of the **substitute** world.

## What remains uncertain

Several major questions remain open.

- **Long-term effects** are still under-studied; many of the strongest experiments are short-term or weeks long rather than years long [[49]](https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/AI%20Companions%20Reduce%20Loneliness%2011.7.2025_57451c02-8047-4e0d-abfc-55841f64166d.pdf)(https://www.hbs.edu/ris/Publication%20Files/AI%20Companions%20Reduce%20Loneliness%2011.7.2025_57451c02-8047-4e0d-abfc-55841f64166d.pdf) [[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.17473v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2503.17473v2) [[90]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-025-02318-6)(https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-025-02318-6).
- It remains unclear **which users benefit** from AI companionship and which are harmed, though youth, isolated users, and high-intensity users appear especially important groups [[118]](https://www.apa.org/topics/artificial-intelligence-machine-learning/health-advisory-ai-adolescent-well-being)(https://www.apa.org/topics/artificial-intelligence-machine-learning/health-advisory-ai-adolescent-well-being) [[122]](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.17473v2)(https://arxiv.org/html/2503.17473v2) [[48]](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.12605v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2506.12605v1).
- The field still lacks strong evidence on **family-level and couple-level causal effects** over time [[90]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-025-02318-6)(https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00146-025-02318-6) [[99]](https://ddss.princeton.edu/research/featured-projects/effect-chatbot-technology-human-social-health)(https://ddss.princeton.edu/research/featured-projects/effect-chatbot-technology-human-social-health).
- We still do not know how far current changes in communication norms—AI drafting, tone coaching, smart replies—will generalize into durable changes in trust and sincerity expectations [[4]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9)(https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9) [[192]](https://news.ufl.edu/2025/08/writing-ai-work/)(https://news.ufl.edu/2025/08/writing-ai-work/) [[193]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12765382/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12765382/).
- Institutional effects likely differ across **class, gender, language, disability, and region**, but the evidence remains uneven and often fragmented [[60]](https://assets.kpmg.com/content/dam/kpmgsites/xx/pdf/2025/05/trust-attitudes-and-use-of-ai-global-report.pdf)(https://assets.kpmg.com/content/dam/kpmgsites/xx/pdf/2025/05/trust-attitudes-and-use-of-ai-global-report.pdf) [[183]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0308596125000941)(https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0308596125000941) [[188]](https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/EJ1472502.pdf)(https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/EJ1472502.pdf) [[191]](https://www.civicus.org/documents/ddi/final-deepfakes-elections-report-civicus-ddi-research-2025.pdf)(https://www.civicus.org/documents/ddi/final-deepfakes-elections-report-civicus-ddi-research-2025.pdf) [[195]](https://womenandtech.indiana.edu/about/news/2025/mind-the-gap.html)(https://womenandtech.indiana.edu/about/news/2025/mind-the-gap.html).

Two additional uncertainties are especially decision-relevant. First, most existing evidence measures self-reported loneliness, satisfaction, or use patterns rather than verified time allocation across human ties. Second, the fastest-changing products are not static enough for old findings to transfer cleanly: memory systems, voice modes, embodied interfaces, and platform integration may materially change attachment intensity and relational spillovers.

## Conclusion

The best current answer is that AI interaction is already influencing interpersonal relations in **three layered ways**.

First, AI is becoming a **quasi-social partner** for some users, especially in companionship, advice, and emotional support [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/)(https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12960742/) [[33]](https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf)(https://www.commonsensemedia.org/sites/default/files/research/report/talk-trust-and-trade-offs_2025_web.pdf) [[48]](https://arxiv.org/html/2506.12605v1)(https://arxiv.org/html/2506.12605v1). Second, AI is becoming a **ubiquitous mediator** of human-human interaction, changing how messages are written, how people are encountered, how performance is evaluated, and how sincerity is judged [[2]](https://socialmedialab.sites.stanford.edu/sites/g/files/sbiybj22976/files/media/file/hancock-jcmc-ai-mediated-communication.pdf)(https://socialmedialab.sites.stanford.edu/sites/g/files/sbiybj22976/files/media/file/hancock-jcmc-ai-mediated-communication.pdf) [[4]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9)(https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30938-9) [[192]](https://news.ufl.edu/2025/08/writing-ai-work/)(https://news.ufl.edu/2025/08/writing-ai-work/) [[194]](https://www.europarl.europa.eu/RegData/etudes/STUD/2025/774670/EPRS_STU(2025)774670_EN.pdf)(https
